name: Guadalajara (Mexico)

colors.symbolism: service to the rulers and the promotion of agriculture (per the heraldic interpretation of the 1539 grant)
doing good to the poor (per the heraldic interpretation of the 1539 grant)

description: Three horizontal stripes in 3:4:3 proportion — dark blue (top), gold (centre), dark blue (bottom) — with the city's coat of arms charged on the central gold stripe.
history: Although the blue-and-gold colours and the city's coat of arms have been used since their grant by Emperor Charles V on 8 November 1539 to the City of Guadalajara and Kingdom of New Galicia, the flag was not formally codified until the Municipal Government Code of Guadalajara — published 29 November 2019 in the Gaceta Municipal — adopted it as the official municipal flag, taking effect 1 January 2020. The proportions are 3/10 for each blue stripe and 4/10 for the central gold stripe.

features.symbolism: dark blue and gold, the colours of the coat of arms granted by Charles V to the City of Guadalajara on 8 November 1539; in the heraldic interpretation, blue signifies service to the rulers and the promotion of agriculture, and gold signifies doing good to the poor

features.role: coat of arms of the City of Guadalajara (granted by Charles V in 1539)
features.symbolism: two gold crowned lions rampant addorsed against a pine tree on a blue field within a red bordure bearing seven gold saltires; the arms were granted by Emperor Charles V on 8 November 1539 to the City of Guadalajara and the Kingdom of New Galicia
